resume:
Director of Academic Innovation & Student Success | Transformational EdTech Leader

- Visionary education strategist leveraging data-driven, learner-centric, outcomes-obsessed methodologies at scale
- Architect of next-generation, AI-powered, personalized learning ecosystems across K-12 and higher-ed verticals
- Thought leader in competency-based, mastery-aligned, future-ready pedagogical frameworks
- Champion of equity-centered, holistic, whole-child student success transformations
- Cross-functional synergy driver partnering with faculty, administrators, and ed-tech stakeholders end-to-end
- Deep fluency in NWEA MAP, i-Ready, formative assessment, learning analytics, and engagement telemetry
- Serial innovator who operationalizes blended, hybrid, flipped, and adaptive learning paradigms
- Frequent keynote speaker on the future of scalable, personalized, equitable education

Most recent: BrightPath Learning — Director of Academic Innovation (2020–present)
Prior: Summit Charter Network — Director of Curriculum & Assessment (2016–2020)
jd: We are seeking a Director of Academic Innovation to lead curriculum, assessment, and learning-outcomes strategy across a 40-school charter network serving 28,000 students. Reporting to the Chief Academic Officer, you will own measurable student-outcome targets: median reading-growth percentile (currently 48, target 60), course-pass rate in gateway math (currently 71%, target 85%), and a $6M annual instructional-technology budget. The role requires a track record of selecting and rolling out an adaptive-learning platform across multiple campuses, demonstrated improvement in standardized growth measures, and experience managing faculty change at scale. You will inherit an underperforming math intervention program and a stalled platform implementation. Strong candidates can walk through a single initiative end to end — the decision, the rollout sequence, and the outcome data that proves it worked.

history.q: Walk me through your current scope at BrightPath — schools, students, budget, the outcome metrics you own.
Your resume says 'AI-powered personalized learning ecosystems.' Name one specific platform you actually deployed.
Across how many campuses did that platform go live, and over what period?
What outcome metric did you expect it to move, and what was the baseline?
Did it move that metric? Give me the before-and-after number.
Let's talk gateway math. What was your intervention, and who designed it?
What was the course-pass rate before and after your intervention?
How did you isolate your intervention's effect from other changes happening that year?
Tell me about the faculty change management — how did you get teachers to actually adopt it?
Was there a campus where adoption failed? What happened there?
On the $6M tech budget — describe one specific reallocation decision you made and why.
What did you cut to fund that, and what was the tradeoff?
Walk me through the stalled platform implementation you'd be inheriting here — have you turned one around before?
On that turnaround, what was week one, week two, week three?
Give me one initiative end to end: the decision, the rollout sequence, and the outcome data that proved it worked.
I need the outcome data specifically. What number changed, by how much, and by when?

candidate_last_answer: Outcome data, yes — but you know what's fascinating, and I think this is the real story here, is how the whole conversation around assessment has evolved over my career. When I started at Summit, we were still arguing about whether standardized testing even measured anything meaningful, and I remember this one faculty retreat where we spent a whole weekend debating the philosophy of mastery. I could honestly write a book about that era. And it ties into my broader thesis about equity, which I've spoken about at three conferences now. So the number — well, growth percentile moved, and it was meaningful, but the deeper point I always come back to is that data alone never tells the human story, and that's really been my life's work if I'm being honest.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: growth percentile moved, and it was meaningful

expected-yield: the exact before-and-after growth-percentile figures for the named platform, the number of campuses and timeframe over which the change occurred, and confirmation it cleared the interviewer's specific request rather than another philosophical detour
